title: How Propaganda Works: The Techniques Used to Shape Public Opinion
body:
Propaganda is not a modern invention. Edward Bernays, the nephew of Sigmund Freud and one of the founders of modern public relations, published a book called Propaganda in 1928 in which he described the 'invisible government' of those who manipulate public opinion — and celebrated it as a necessary feature of democratic society. His techniques, drawn from his uncle's psychology, are still in use nearly a century later.

The first mechanism is emotional activation. Propaganda works not by providing arguments to be evaluated by reason but by generating emotional responses that bypass rational evaluation. Fear is the most potent: a population in a state of fear is significantly more susceptible to accepting the authority of those who claim to offer protection, and significantly less capable of the critical evaluation that might question whether the threat is real or whether the offered protection is the right response.

Repetition is the second mechanism. The illusory truth effect — the finding that repeated exposure to a claim increases its perceived truthfulness, independently of the claim's actual accuracy — is one of the most robust in cognitive psychology. It works even when the audience knows the claim is being repeated for persuasive purposes, and even when they are given accurate information that contradicts it. Repetition, at sufficient scale and frequency, creates a felt sense of familiarity that the brain interprets as evidence of truth.

In-group/out-group dynamics are the third. Identifying a group as threatening — whether defined by religion, ethnicity, political affiliation, or any other characteristic — activates tribal psychology that is largely resistant to factual correction. Once a group has been identified as the enemy, information that supports this identification is readily accepted while information that challenges it is dismissed as coming from compromised sources.

title: The Concentration of Media Ownership: What It Means for Democracy
body:
In 1983, approximately fifty corporations controlled the majority of the media in the United States. By 2012, that number had reduced to six. In the UK, three companies control approximately 70% of national newspaper circulation. Globally, the past three decades have seen significant consolidation of media ownership into the hands of a declining number of large corporations and billionaire proprietors.

The implications for democratic function are contested but significant. The argument that ownership concentration matters for content rests on the observation that proprietors have interests — financial, political, regulatory — that can and do influence editorial decisions. The Leveson Inquiry in the UK documented the relationship between newspaper proprietors, politicians, and police in ways that raised serious questions about the independence of editorial decision-making from commercial and political interest.

The counterargument is that media pluralism has actually increased with the internet — that the decline of traditional media concentration has been more than offset by the proliferation of online sources, giving audiences access to a far wider range of perspectives than was available in the era of three television channels and a handful of newspapers.

Both arguments contain truth. The internet has genuinely expanded access to diverse perspectives. It has also created new concentrations of power — in the platform companies that control the algorithms through which most people encounter information — that present their own, distinctive challenges to democratic information environments. The replacement of a small number of proprietor-controlled newspapers by a small number of algorithm-controlled platforms may not represent an unambiguous improvement in the quality of the information environment.

title: Why Democracies Fail: What Political Science Teaches About Democratic Backsliding
body:
Democratic backsliding — the gradual erosion of democratic norms, institutions, and practices — has become one of the central concerns of contemporary political science. The pattern is now well-documented across cases from Hungary and Poland to Turkey, Brazil, and the United States: in each case, elected leaders with majoritarian mandates have used legal and institutional means to entrench their power, weaken checks and balances, and reduce the meaningful competitiveness of future elections.

The distinction that Steven Levitsky and Daniel Ziblatt draw in How Democracies Die is instructive: most contemporary democratic failures do not occur through military coups or sudden violent ruptures, but through the slow, incremental erosion of democratic norms by elected leaders who maintain a procedural veneer of democratic legitimacy while systematically undermining its substance. The judiciary is packed with loyalists. Independent regulatory agencies are staffed with political allies. The press is pressured, marginalised, or bought. Electoral rules are changed to advantage the incumbent. Each step is defensible in isolation; the cumulative effect is the hollowing out of democratic accountability.

The most important guardrails, Levitsky and Ziblatt argue, are not constitutional provisions but informal norms — particularly the mutual toleration of political opponents as legitimate competitors rather than existential enemies, and the institutional forbearance to refrain from using legal but constitutionally destabilising tactics even when one has the power to do so. When these norms erode, constitutional provisions prove surprisingly fragile.

The historical evidence suggests that the most robust democracies are those with strong civil society organisations, independent judiciary, genuinely free press, and — critically — political elites in all major parties who share a commitment to the democratic system's survival above their own partisan advantage.

title: Universal Basic Income: The Arguments For and Against
body:
Universal Basic Income — a regular, unconditional cash payment from the government to all citizens, regardless of employment status or income — is among the most debated policy proposals of the early twenty-first century. Supported by thinkers and politicians across the political spectrum (from Milton Friedman's negative income tax to contemporary left progressives), it has moved from a fringe idea to a mainstream policy debate in the context of growing inequality and anxiety about technological unemployment.

The case for UBI rests on several arguments. It would eliminate the poverty trap created by means-tested benefits, where recipients face effective marginal tax rates above 70% as benefits are withdrawn when they earn more. It would provide genuine economic security that enables risk-taking — starting a business, leaving an exploitative job, retraining. It would recognise the value of unpaid care work. And it might provide a floor of economic security in an economy increasingly disrupted by automation.

The case against is equally serious. The cost of a genuinely adequate UBI, at the scale of a developed economy, is very large — requiring either significant tax increases or the replacement of existing social programmes whose beneficiaries may be worse off under a universal but less targeted system. The inflationary effects are contested but real concerns. And the empirical evidence from pilot programmes, while generally positive on wellbeing outcomes, does not yet resolve the macroeconomic questions at national scale.

What the debate reveals is a deeper disagreement about the purpose of the welfare state — whether it should be a safety net for those who cannot work, or a social floor for all citizens regardless of their market income.

title: Climate Politics: Why the Gap Between Knowledge and Action Persists
body:
The scientific consensus on climate change is overwhelming and has been for decades. The gap between that scientific consensus and the policy response — in most countries, inadequate to prevent significant disruption — is one of the most consequential political failures in human history. Understanding why requires looking beyond individual denial or ignorance to the structural features of the political economy of climate policy.

The temporal mismatch is fundamental. Climate change is a slow-moving crisis whose most severe consequences lie decades in the future, while electoral cycles are measured in years and politicians' career incentives are calibrated to those cycles. Policies that impose immediate, visible costs to prevent future, diffuse harms are structurally difficult for democratic systems to sustain, particularly when the future harms fall predominantly on people who are not yet voters.

The concentration of interests is the second structural problem. The industries that produce fossil fuels, and the broader economic sectors that depend on cheap fossil energy, have concentrated and immediate interests in preventing climate policy. The interests on the other side — everyone who benefits from a stable climate — are diffuse, difficult to organise, and not yet mobilised by the experience of acute harm in the way that is typically required to generate the political pressure that produces policy change.

The international collective action problem adds a further layer: any single country that implements ambitious climate policy bears immediate domestic costs while the benefits are shared globally. This creates rational incentives for free-riding — waiting for others to act while hoping to benefit from their action — that international climate agreements have proven only partially successful at overcoming.

title: How Social Movements Actually Change Things
body:
Social movements — organised, sustained efforts by ordinary citizens to change political, social, or economic arrangements — are the primary mechanism by which democratic societies undergo significant change outside of electoral politics. The civil rights movement, the suffragette movement, the labour movement, the environmental movement: the major social transformations of the twentieth century were driven by organised civic action, often in the face of significant institutional resistance.

Political scientists studying movement success and failure have identified several consistent factors. First, the distinction between radical flanks and mainstream wings: successful movements typically have both. The more radical wing makes visible the extent of injustice and pressure for change; the more moderate wing provides the negotiations and institutional relationships through which actual policy change is achieved. The two wings are often in tension and sometimes actively hostile to each other, but both tend to be necessary.

Second, the importance of disruption: movements that operate entirely within conventional channels — lobbying, electoral campaigning, media engagement — are significantly less effective at achieving change than those that also employ forms of non-violent disruption that impose costs on those with power to change the situation. The civil rights movement's lunch counter sit-ins and freedom rides imposed direct economic and reputational costs on the systems they challenged.

Third, the role of defection among elites: major social changes typically require not only sustained movement pressure but the defection of significant portions of the elite that originally supported or tolerated the status quo. Police who refuse to suppress demonstrations, business leaders who support reform, politicians who break with party discipline — these defections are often decisive in determining whether movements succeed or stall.

title: How Democracies Die: Historical Patterns
body:
The democratic backsliding of the past decade has generated a substantial body of political science literature attempting to identify the patterns by which democracies erode. The central finding of Steven Levitsky and Daniel Ziblatt's influential analysis is that contemporary democratic decay rarely happens through military coup or sudden revolutionary seizure of power. It happens through the actions of elected leaders who systematically dismantle the institutional constraints on executive authority: packing the courts with loyalists, attacking the independence of the press, weaponising state institutions against political opponents, and gradually delegitimising the opposition as enemies rather than adversaries.

The historical examples are instructive. Mussolini did not dissolve Italian parliamentary democracy on the first day he took power. He spent three years accumulating power incrementally, using the institutions of democracy to undermine their own independence, before the formal abolition of opposition parties in 1926. Hugo Chávez in Venezuela, Recep Tayyip Erdoğan in Turkey, and Viktor Orbán in Hungary all followed variants of the same pattern: electoral success, followed by institutional capture, followed by the gradual reduction of competitive elections to performances whose outcomes were increasingly predetermined.

What this pattern analysis suggests for the defence of democracy is both hopeful and demanding. Hopeful because democratic decay is typically a gradual process that can be interrupted if recognised and resisted at an early stage; demanding because it requires sustained engagement from democratic citizens, institutions, and political parties willing to put democratic norms above short-term partisan advantage. The courts, the free press, and civil society organisations that maintain the accountability infrastructure of democracy require active defence.

title: Understanding Inequality: Causes and Consequences
body:
Economic inequality has increased in most developed countries since the 1980s, and the political consequences — the populist revolts, the institutional distrust, the polarisation of political life — are among the defining features of our current moment. Understanding why inequality has increased requires distinguishing between several contributing factors: technological change that has increased the returns to high-skill labour while reducing demand for routine cognitive and physical tasks; the globalisation of manufacturing supply chains; the decline of labour unions; and deliberate policy choices on tax, education, and social insurance that have varied significantly between countries with similar exposure to the first three factors.

The consequences of high inequality extend beyond the economic to the social and political. Richard Wilkinson and Kate Pickett's research demonstrates that across a wide range of social outcomes — physical and mental health, educational attainment, crime, social mobility, substance abuse — more equal societies perform substantially better than less equal ones, controlling for overall wealth. The mechanism appears to be not merely the poverty of the poor but the social and psychological consequences of steep hierarchy: the chronic stress of status competition, the erosion of social trust, and the political exclusion of those at the bottom.

The political economy of inequality reduction is formidably complex. The policies that have most reliably compressed inequality — progressive taxation, strong collective bargaining, universal access to quality education and healthcare — face sustained opposition from those who benefit from inequality and have the political influence that wealth provides. Countries like the Nordic states demonstrate that high inequality is not an inevitable consequence of advanced capitalism; it is a policy choice, and the consequences of that choice are borne primarily by those who had the least say in making it.

title: Media Literacy in the Age of Misinformation
body:
The information environment of the early twenty-first century presents challenges for which neither evolutionary nor cultural history has prepared us. The human brain evolved in an information environment of radical scarcity, in which the primary sources of information were direct sensory experience and the testimony of known individuals within a small community. The psychological shortcuts that served us well in that environment — trusting information that is familiar, that comes from authoritative-seeming sources, that is emotionally vivid, that confirms what we already believe — are systematically exploited by an information ecosystem designed to maximise engagement rather than to inform.

Misinformation spreads faster and further than accurate information on social media platforms, a finding documented by MIT's Sinan Aral and colleagues in a large-scale study of social media data. The mechanism appears to be emotional arousal: false information tends to be more novel and more emotionally provocative than accurate information, and novelty and emotion drive sharing. This creates an asymmetric environment in which the purveyors of misinformation have a structural advantage over those communicating accurate but less emotionally charged information.

Media literacy — the capacity to evaluate sources, understand the incentive structures of media institutions, distinguish between news, opinion, and propaganda, and recognise the psychological mechanisms being exploited in any given piece of content — is an educational priority that has yet to receive the institutional investment its importance warrants. The most effective media literacy interventions focus not on specific fact-checks but on the transferable skill of recognising manipulation techniques. 'Inoculation theory' — pre-emptively exposing people to weakened versions of misleading techniques alongside explanations of how they work — has shown promising results across a politically diverse population.

title: Climate Change: The Policy Challenge
body:
The scientific case for anthropogenic climate change is as well-established as science gets: the evidence from multiple independent lines of inquiry — atmospheric temperature records, ocean heat content, sea level rise, ice core data, satellite observations — is mutually consistent, and the attribution of this warming to greenhouse gas emissions from human activity is supported by basic physics confirmed over a century of laboratory and atmospheric science. The scientific debate is not about whether climate change is happening or whether humans are causing it. It is about rates, feedbacks, regional variations, and tipping points — questions of degree and detail rather than of fundamental mechanism.

The policy challenge is considerably more complex than the science. It involves genuine tradeoffs between present consumption and future welfare, between the interests of currently industrialising nations and those that industrialised earlier, between the economic interests of those who own fossil fuel assets and those of everyone else, and between the short time horizons of democratic electoral cycles and the long time horizons over which climate policy has its effects. These tradeoffs do not have technically correct answers — they are distributional questions that reflect value judgments about whose interests matter and over what time horizon.

The practical progress being made — the dramatic cost reductions in renewable energy, the growth of electric vehicles, the expansion of carbon markets, the increasing integration of climate risk into financial regulation — is real and encouraging. So is the gap between what is being achieved and what the science says is necessary. Holding both of these things clearly in mind — neither dismissing the progress nor overstating it — is the intellectually honest position, and it is the position from which the most productive civic engagement becomes possible.

title: The Welfare State: Origins and Future
body:
The welfare state — the set of government programmes providing income support, healthcare, education, and other social insurance to citizens — emerged in its modern form in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, primarily in response to the social dislocations produced by industrialisation and urbanisation. Bismarck's introduction of health insurance and old-age pensions in Germany in the 1880s was explicitly designed to pre-empt socialist political organisation by giving workers a stake in the existing political order. The British welfare state, consolidated by the Attlee government after the Second World War, was shaped by Beveridge's diagnosis of the 'five giants' — want, disease, ignorance, squalor, and idleness — that stood in the way of human flourishing.

The welfare state has delivered remarkable results across its history. Child poverty rates in countries with strong welfare systems are dramatically lower than in those with weaker ones. Life expectancy, educational attainment, and social mobility are all positively associated with welfare state generosity, controlling for national income. The post-war decades in which the welfare state was built and expanded in most Western democracies were decades of broadly shared prosperity and declining inequality — a conjunction that is not coincidental.

The pressures on the welfare state in the twenty-first century are real: ageing populations increasing demand for health and pension spending, labour market changes eroding the contribution base, and political polarisation making reform more difficult. The question is not whether the welfare state must adapt but how it can adapt while preserving its core functions. This is one of the central political questions of our time, and how it is answered will determine the kind of society the next generation inherits.

title: Globalisation: Promise and Discontents
body:
Globalisation — the integration of national economies through trade, capital flows, migration, and the diffusion of technology and ideas — has been the dominant economic phenomenon of the past forty years. Its aggregate effects have been remarkable: the share of the world's population living in extreme poverty has fallen from over thirty percent in 1990 to under ten percent today, a reduction in absolute deprivation unprecedented in human history. This achievement is substantially the product of global economic integration, particularly through the incorporation of China and other Asian economies into global manufacturing supply chains.

The distribution of these gains has been deeply unequal in ways that created the political backlash now reshaping the international order. Within developed economies, the gains from globalisation accrued disproportionately to the highly educated, the owners of capital, and consumers of tradeable goods whose prices fell. The costs — in manufacturing job losses, in community dislocation, in the erosion of the social power of labour — were borne disproportionately by working-class communities in industrialised regions. The political alienation of these communities from the mainstream parties that oversaw and defended globalisation is one of the key facts of contemporary politics.

The question of what comes after the liberal international economic order is genuinely open. The reshoring of critical supply chains, the increasing use of trade policy as geopolitical instrument, and the rise of industrial policy as a legitimate tool of economic strategy are all reshaping the rules. Whether the result is a more equitable form of international economic integration or a more fragmented, zero-sum competition between great powers depends partly on decisions that governments and citizens are making right now.

title: The Politics of Identity: A Careful Analysis
body:
Identity politics — the organisation of political action around the shared identities of groups defined by race, gender, sexuality, religion, or other characteristics — has been both the most energising and the most divisive development in Western politics over the past thirty years. Its defenders argue that it simply names what politics has always been: the organised advocacy of groups whose interests have been excluded from the mainstream. Its critics argue that it substitutes cultural signalling for material politics, fragments coalitions that could otherwise achieve structural change, and produces a politics of grievance that crowds out the universalist claims that have historically been necessary for broad progressive coalitions.

Both of these positions contain genuine insights. The organised advocacy of marginalised groups has produced real, lasting, and valuable changes in law, culture, and institutional practice: the rights of women, LGBTQ+ people, and racial minorities in most developed democracies are dramatically better than they were fifty years ago, partly because of sustained political organisation around shared identity. At the same time, the turn toward expressive identity politics and away from distributional economic politics has coincided with a period of increasing economic inequality, suggesting that the two may be in tension in ways that matter for outcomes.

The most productive analytical position neither dismisses identity politics as mere distraction nor embraces it uncritically as the whole of progressive politics. It recognises that the recognition of dignity and the distribution of material resources are both genuine political goods, and that a politics adequate to the present moment needs to pursue both — finding the common interests that can unite diverse groups rather than insisting on the specificity of experience that divides them.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Society: The Policy Questions
body:
The governance of artificial intelligence is one of the most urgent and least resolved policy challenges of our time. The technology is developing faster than regulatory frameworks, as is typical of transformative technologies, and the potential consequences — in employment, in political information environments, in national security, in the concentration of economic power — are large enough to warrant serious institutional attention. The challenge is compounded by the genuinely uncertain nature of AI's development trajectory: the range of credible expert opinion about what will be possible in ten years is wider than for almost any other technology.

The most immediate policy questions concern liability and accountability. When an AI system causes harm — through a medical misdiagnosis, a discriminatory credit decision, a self-driving vehicle accident — who bears responsibility? The existing legal frameworks for product liability and negligence do not map cleanly onto AI systems whose behaviour is probabilistic, whose decision-making processes are often opaque, and whose performance can degrade in ways that are difficult to anticipate. Developing new legal frameworks that create appropriate incentives for safety and transparency without stifling beneficial innovation is one of the central regulatory challenges.

The longer-term questions concern power concentration. The development of frontier AI systems requires computational resources and talent concentrated in a small number of very large technology companies, predominantly American and Chinese. This concentration creates risks of monopoly power, of regulatory capture, and of systems whose design reflects the values and interests of their developers in ways that may not be broadly beneficial. The international dimension adds further complexity: the geopolitical competition between the US and China in AI development is reshaping the international order in ways whose full implications are not yet visible.

title: Housing: Why It's So Hard to Get Right
body:
Housing — the question of how to ensure that people have access to adequate shelter at affordable cost — is one of the most persistent and most politically contentious challenges in contemporary public policy. In most large cities in the developed world, housing costs have risen dramatically relative to incomes over the past three decades, pricing younger generations out of the ownership that their parents achieved, consuming increasing shares of household income, and concentrating the benefits of urban productivity growth in the hands of existing property owners. The economic and social consequences are significant: reduced labour mobility, increased wealth inequality, declining household formation, and the concentration of the most severe deprivation in the most inadequate housing stock.

The causes of the housing crisis are reasonably well-understood, even if the politics of addressing them are difficult. In most expensive cities, the supply of housing — the number of units available — has not kept pace with demand. This is primarily a consequence of planning and land use regulation that restricts the density and type of development permitted, effectively giving existing residents (who tend to be property owners with incentives to restrict supply) a veto over new development that would benefit future residents. The economic analysis is clear: where you allow more housing to be built, housing costs tend to be lower. Where you restrict supply, they tend to be higher.

The political difficulty is that the beneficiaries of housing supply restriction are concentrated, organised, and powerful, while the beneficiaries of supply expansion are diffuse and often not yet present in the jurisdiction. The homeowner whose property value depends on scarcity has every incentive to mobilise against new development. The young person who might be able to afford to live in the city if more housing were built is not yet there to make the case. Breaking this political equilibrium is one of the most important and most difficult tasks in urban policy, and the cities that are figuring out how to do it are worth watching carefully.

title: The Future of Work: Automation and Human Possibility
body:
Every wave of technological automation in history has been accompanied by predictions that this time, unlike previous times, the machines will take all the jobs. So far, every one of these predictions has been wrong — not because automation has not displaced workers in specific occupations, which it has, but because the economy has consistently generated new kinds of work faster than old kinds have been eliminated. The historians' question is whether this historical pattern will continue with artificial intelligence, which is qualitatively different from previous automation in its applicability to cognitive as well as physical tasks.

The honest answer is that nobody knows. The economic models that predict modest overall employment effects rely on assumptions about the emergence of new sectors and tasks that may or may not be realised. The models that predict dramatic displacement rely on assumptions about the rate of AI capability improvement and the cost of deployment that are also uncertain. What is clear is that the transition — whatever its ultimate scale — will be uneven: some occupations, industries, and regions will be far more affected than others, and the costs and benefits will not be distributed proportionally to those who most benefit from AI's productivity gains.

The policy responses that are most robustly supported by evidence focus less on preventing automation — which is both impossible and would forgo genuine productivity gains — and more on ensuring that the transition is managed in ways that share the benefits broadly and support those who bear the costs. Strong social insurance, active labour market policies that support retraining and transition, and the taxation of productivity gains in ways that fund these supports are the elements of a coherent response. The political will to implement such a response is, in most countries, the limiting factor.
